macro_segments.title: Rav's Statement on Timing of Payment for Kefel versus Keren

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rav's Principle: Kefel and Four-Five Based on Court Assessment, Keren Based on Theft
Gemara's Limitations on Rav's Statement: Market Value Changes and Slaughter

macro_segments.title: The Baraita of Rabbi Chanina Supporting Rav Through the Case of False Oath on Stolen Object

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Bailee Falsely Swears Object Was Stolen, Then Admits
Case One: Admission Before Witnesses Arrive—No Kefel, Only Korban and Chomesh
Case Two: Admission After Witnesses Arrive—Both Kefel and Korban/Chomesh Obligations
Case Three: Oath Then Admission—Payment of Kefel and Kefel Includes or Excludes Chomesh (Dispute of Rabbi Yaakov vs. Sages vs. Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai)

macro_segments.title: Debate on Whether Chomesh (Fifth) Can Be Included in or Is Distinct from Kefel

micro_segments.title: Gemara's Question: If Value Stays Constant, How Can Chomesh Equal Kefel?
Resolution: Case Where Value Decreases, Making Kefel and Chomesh Equivalent in Amount
Rava's Counter-Explanation: Multiple Oaths Create Multiple Chomeshim Equaling One Kefel Without Proving Rav
Consensus: Only Principal Payment Obligates Chomesh, Not Kefel
Dispute Between Sages and Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai on Whether Asham Korban Is Brought with Kefel

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Kinyanei Gneivah: Conceptual Models and Types of Acquisition by Thieves

micro_segments.title: Two Conceptual Models: Act of Taking Possession vs. Removal of Return Obligation
Shinuy Ma'aseh: Transformation of Object (Wood to Box, Wool to Garment)
Shinuy Hashem: Natural Change of Name (Calf to Ox, Lamb to Ram)
Yeush: Owner Gives Up Hope—Passive Possession vs. Active Act of Kinyan
Yeush v'Shinuy Reshut: Change of Location Combining Loss of Hope

macro_segments.title: Case of Stolen Calf That Becomes an Ox: Does Shinuy Hashem Cause Acquisition?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ila's Position: Natural Shinuy Causes Acquisition, So No Kefel or Four-Five
Challenge: Baraita Says You Pay Four-Five Even When Calf Becomes Ox, Contradicting Rabbi Ila
Rabbi Ila's Response: Different Object Means No Acquisition, Yet No Four-Five Because Object Changed
Exchange of Rejections: Both Positions Called Objectionable by the Other

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Zeira's Challenge: Does Shinuy Hashem Create a New Name Warranting Acquisition?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Zeira Asks: Doesn't Different Name Constitute Shinuy Hashem for Acquisition?
Rava's Response: Even One-Day-Old Animals Have Their Category Name, So Not a True Shinuy Hashem
Continuation of Contradiction: Baraita Still Contradicts Rabbi Ila Even After Rava's Answer

macro_segments.title: Rav Sheshet's Resolution: The Baraita Reflects Beit Shammai's View That Shinuy Does Not Create Acquisition

micro_segments.title: Beit Shammai's Position: Shinuy Stays in Its Place—No Acquisition, No New Object
Beit Shammai vs. Beit Hillel Debate on Prostitute's Fee: Whether Changed Form Is Forbidden
Beit Shammai's Proof from 'Gam': Both Prohibited Even After Transformation
Beit Hillel's Counter: 'Hem' and 'Lo Shinuyyeihem' Show Changed Form Is Separate Object

macro_segments.title: Conceptual Foundation: Recognizing Objects as Changed Before Discussing Kinyan

micro_segments.title: Principle: Only When Object Is Recognized as Different Can Acquisition Through Shinuy Apply
Broader Implication: Beit Shammai Refuses to Recognize Changes; Beit Hillel Acknowledges Them

macro_segments.title: Rava's Answer to Kefel/Keren Timing Issue Using the Principle of Identity of Stolen Object

micro_segments.title: Rava's Four-Word Answer: Distinguishing Between Original Object Value and Current Value
Explanation via Tosafot: You Pay Multiple of the Object You Stole, Not a New Object
Alternative Rashi Explanation: Units of Payment—Objects vs. Cash

macro_segments.title: Rabbah's Evidence That Shinuy Causes Acquisition From Torah and Mishnah

micro_segments.title: Biblical Source: 'Heshiv et Hagezeila Asher Gazal'—Object Must Be in Original Form
Mishnaic Source: Stolen Wood Made into Vessels, Wool into Garments—Pay Original Value
Additional Source: Dyeing Wool Before Giving to Kohen Exempts from Obligation

macro_segments.title: Yeush as Acquisition Method: Uncertainty Whether Biblical or Rabbinic

micro_segments.title: Rabbah Acknowledges Yeush Works but Questions Its Basis: Biblical or Rabbinic
Parallel to Lost Object: Owner Gives Up Hope Before Finder Takes Possession
Difference from Theft: Yeush Occurs After Thief Already Has Possession
Gemara's Analysis: When Yeush Works for Lost Objects vs. When It Might Not for Theft
Proposal: Yeush Is Rabbinic for Theft to Encourage Repentance

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Opposing View: Yeush by Itself Does Not Effect Acquisition

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef: Yeush Alone Does Not Work Even Rabbinically—Needs Third-Party Transfer
Acquisition Only Through Yeush V'Shinuy Reshut: Third Party Gets It After Yeush
Logic: Thief's Pre-Yeush Possession Prevents Yeush Alone from Severing Connection

topical_tags.term: Asham korban (sin offering for theft via oath)
